text: The surgeon observes a slightly fatty liver during the laparoscopic left lateral sectionectomy.
The video shows a cystic lesion located in segment three of the liver.
The surgeon begins the procedure by taking down the round ligament, which is visible as a fibrous cord structure in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon carefully separates tissue from the parietal wall, ensuring that free fat does not obstruct the view between the camera and the resection surface.
The surgeon takes down the falciform ligament, followed by the dissection of the right coronary and triangular ligaments, as part of the hepatectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses pneumodissection to separate the triangular ligament, progressing towards the coronary ligament. Slight traction is applied to the left collateral section to fully take down the left coronary ligament.
The surgeon continues dissecting the triangular ligament, progressively exposing the left side of the bare area of the liver while avoiding exposure of the right side.
The surgeon reflects the left collateral section upward, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the crest of the diaphragm behind it.
The surgeon opens the past flaccida of the aceromentum, entering the space to continue the dissection.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the accessory left hepatic artery from the left gastric artery, progressing the dissection until reaching the superior surface of the liver.
The surgical team prepares for the Pringle maneuver, which involves occluding the hepatoduodenal ligament to control blood flow to the liver.
The surgeon uses a modified Huygens maneuver, passing a grasper through the foramina of Winslow, visible in the opening made in the past Placida.

A cut Foley's catheter is inserted through the opening, with its cut end positioned inside. This setup allows for the Pringle maneuver to be performed when needed by tightening the catheter and applying a clip.
In the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon uses a rubber band technique for retraction, applying it similarly to the open technique, to maintain exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a rubber band technique for dynamic retraction during parenchymal transection, following the concept of 'opening the book.' A suture is passed at an appropriate location on the left side, while the right side utilizes the down ligament for retraction. The rubber band is adjusted to provide dynamic traction as the transection progresses.
The surgeon dissects and removes the liver parenchyma located between segment four and segment three on the inferior surface.

The surgeon initiates parenchymal transection slightly to the left of the falciform ligament, using a harmonic scalpel aided by bipolar forceps. The central venous pressure (CVP) is maintained below two during this phase of the procedure.

The surgeon uses a combination of bipolar forceps and harmonic scalpel to slowly transect the liver parenchyma. The active jaw of the harmonic scalpel is inserted into the tissue, followed by gradual closure of the inactive jaw to complete the transection, maintaining a dry surgical field.
The surgeon scores the liver tissue to the maximum posterior level before slowly beginning the transection of the depth surface.
The surgeon identifies two or three crossing branches of the left hepatic vein and the portal vein system in the superior aspect of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a silk suture to hook a left hepatic venous branch traversing between segments three and four. A hemlock clip is applied on either side of the vein, facilitating dramatic dissection with the golden finger instrument.

The surgeon removes the silk thread used for hooking before applying the second hemlock clip, allowing the liver to open further. The parenchymal transection is resumed slowly, using bipolar cautery to address minor bleeding and small vessels, while checking for larger branches.

The surgeon progresses posteriorly, revealing a branch likely belonging to the segment IV A portal vein or crossing portal veins between segments II and III. Before continuing further, the segment II and III pedicle is stapled down.

The surgeon carefully turns the tissue towards the left side to avoid stapling the umbilical portion of the left portal vein. After stapling, the segment two and three portal vein plate is visibly taken down together, revealing approximately six centimeters of remaining parenchyma.
The surgeon uses the golden finger instrument to hook around intersegmental veins during posterior and cranial dissection, carefully manipulating these veins for further exposure.

The surgeon uses a hemolog clip to take down the veins between segments II and IV A, ensuring the clip is applied only on the non-specimen side. As the parenchymal transection progresses, bleeding is observed from a buttonhole in the left hepatic vein, prompting rapid action to address the issue and prevent air embolism.

The surgeon elevates the head end of the patient and uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the ligamentum venosum. The ductus venosum is taken down, along with two or three crossing veins and a small segment II vein from the umbilical portion of the portal vein, using Hemlock clips.
The surgeon narrows the parenchyma around the left hepatic vein, revealing the vein with a small buttonhole. The pneumoperitoneum is slightly raised, reducing oozing from the surgical site.

The surgeon uses a 60 mm white stapler to staple the left hepatic vein, leaving minimal parenchyma posteriorly. The remaining parenchyma is taken down using harmonic forceps, completing the left lateral sectionectomy.
The transection surface of the liver is visible, showing no signs of bile leakage or bleeding. Hemostasis has been successfully achieved.

The surgeon shows the stapled segment two and three portal pedicle using a tan 45 mm tri-staple, alongside the stapled left hepatic vein. A rubber band assists in bringing the incision out through the finance deal incision.
